cards.back: The ability to read from or write to any position in a file by moving the file pointer, rather than reading/writing sequentially from beginning to end.

cards.text: To move the read position in a file, use {{c1::seekg()}} (get position). To move the write position, use {{c2::seekp()}} (put position).
The three seek positions are: {{c1::std::ios::beg}} (beginning), {{c2::std::ios::cur}} (current position), and {{c3::std::ios::end}} (end of file).

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.answer: Moves the read position to the end of the file (0 bytes from the end).
cards.explanation: This is commonly used to get file size by then calling tellg().

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.answer: Moves the read position to 10 bytes from the beginning of the file.
cards.explanation: Positive offset moves forward from the reference point.

cards.text: To get the current read position, use {{c1::file.tellg()}}. To get the current write position, use {{c2::file.tellp()}}.

cards.question: How do you get the size of a file?
cards.code:
std::ifstream file("data.txt", std::ios::binary);
// Get file size
cards.explanation: Seek to end, get position (which equals file size), then seek back to beginning for reading.

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.answer: Moves the read position 5 bytes backward from the current position.
cards.explanation: Negative offset moves backward from the reference point.

cards.front: Why is random access more commonly used with binary files than text files?
cards.back: Binary files have fixed-size records, making it easy to calculate positions. Text files have variable-length lines and platform-specific line endings, making position calculations unreliable.

cards.text: To read binary data, use {{c1::file.read(buffer, size)}}. To write binary data, use {{c2::file.write(buffer, size)}}.

cards.concept: When to use random file access
cards.explanation:
Random access is useful for:
  • Database-like files with fixed-size records
  • Reading/modifying specific parts of large files
  • Implementing file-based data structures
  • Avoiding loading entire file into memory
Not recommended for text files with variable-length lines.
examples: struct Record { int id; char name[20]; };

cards.front: What's the difference between seekg and seekp?
cards.back: seekg() moves the get (read) position, seekp() moves the put (write) position. For files opened for both reading and writing, these can be different.
